attributes.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a two month history of pain in her right foot that gets worse with walking or jumping. The pain has got worse over the two weeks since she started morning runs in some tight-fitting shoes. A general examination shows normal findings. On examination of the right foot, there is tenderness in the third intermetatarsal space and compression of the forefoot in the mediolateral direction, while palpating the affected space gives a clicking feeling. An MRI is performed and the section shows the area of the foot where the tenderness is elicited (image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The scan shows a neuroma in the third intermetatarsal space, as outline by the arrow (Image). This is a Morton neuroma, which is a compressive neuropathy of the interdigital nerve in the forefoot. This has developed due to compression and constant irritation at the plantar aspect of the transverse intermetatarsal ligament. Forefoot pain located in the third intermetatarsal space that is reproduced by compressing the interdigital space, mulder's click (compression of the forefoot in the mediolateral direction while palpating the affected space gives clicking feeling) and MRI finding of a faintly enhancing mass in the third intermetatarsal space gives a diagnosis of Morton's neuroma.

Metatarsal stress fractures (March fractures) result from repetitive stress to the forefoot, usually from running, jumping, dancing, and other repetitive weight-bearing activities, which is a possibility in this woman, but it would have resulted in point tenderness over a particular metatarsal shaft, and an MRI would have shown significant soft tissue oedema and bone marrow oedema at an early stage and fractures later. There is no evidence of any stress fractures.

Ganglion cysts, a common benign cystic lesion containing gelatinous material, generally arising from a tendon sheath or joint capsule. These are commonly found in both the feet and the wrists. The MRI would show an unilocular or multilocular rounded or lobular fluid signal mass, adjacent to a joint or tendon sheath. They are often clinically palpable and visible within the foot.

Osteosarcoma of the metatarsal presents as a swelling with mild pain that is often noticed while wearing shoes. The pain, though mild, is often of longer duration. An MRI will reveal a bone lesion with or without bone and surrounding soft tissue destruction, but confirmation in most cases can be done only after biopsy. This is a rare condition.
learningPoints: Morton neuroma is a compressive neuropathy of the interdigital nerve in the forefoot due to compression and constant irritation at the plantar aspect of the transverse intermetatarsal ligament.
Third intermetatarsal space is the most common site of Morton's neuroma.
Incidence is at least five times higher in females when compared to males.
references: Mak MS, Chowdhury R, Johnson R. Morton's neuroma: review of anatomy, pathomechanism, and imaging. Clin Radiol. 2021 Mar;76(3):235.e15-235.e23.
Santiago FR, Muñoz PT, Pryest P, Martínez AM, Olleta NP. Role of imaging methods in diagnosis and treatment of Morton's neuroma. World J Radiol. 2018 Sep 28;10(9):91-99.
Shindle MK, Endo Y, Warren RF, et al. Stress fractures about the tibia, foot, and ankle. J Am Acad Orthop Surg 2012; 20:167.
Edenfield KM, Michaudet C, Nicolette GW, Carek PJ. Foot and Ankle Conditions: Midfoot and Forefoot Conditions. FP Essent. 2018 Feb;465:30-34.
Lee EY, Seeger LL, Nelson SD, Eckardt JJ. Primary osteosarcoma of a metatarsal bone. Skeletal Radiol. 2000 Aug;29(8):474-6. doi: 10.1007/s002560000237. PMID: 11026717.
Cheng SH, Wang TG. A 51-Year-Old Woman with Right Dorsal Foot Pain. J Med Ultrasound. 2019 Jun 4;27(3):162-163. doi: 10.4103/JMU.JMU_110_18. PMID: 31867184; PMCID: PMC6905269.
Munir U, Tafti D, Morgan S. Morton Neuroma [Internet]. National Library of Medicine. StatPearls Publishing LLC; 2022 [cited 2023Mar15]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470249/
